ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hainanese Curry Rice. Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor. Hainanese Curry Rice is a Singaporean composite dish of steamed white rice served with curry sauce, braised pork gravy, pork chop, and stewed cabbage.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish — mixed rice dish with meat and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Hainanese Curry Rice. Code A00GJ (mixed rice dish with meat and sauce) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category for a composite rice-based dish with multiple protein and sauce components. Used as closest_parent; no exact match exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Hainanese Curry Rice or a sufficiently close composite Singaporean rice dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Hainanese Curry Rice weighs approximately 450 g and consists of steamed white rice generously drenched with curry sauce and braised pork gravy, accompanied by a breaded pork chop and stewed cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Calorie and sodium counts of Singaporean hawker foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar, calcium, iron, potassium, magnesium, cholesterol) are sourced from the HPB Singapore food composition database for hawker foods at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (4.44 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100 g) were originally flagged in the input as sourced from a delivery platform at confidence 0.70; these values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable pork-and-rice composite dishes and are retained as best available estimates pending direct HPB verification — flagged in notes. Micronutrient values (phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, and all vitamins) are pipeline-enriched estimates at confidence 0.90, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for comparable ingredients, and are documented as indicative only.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice and mixed rice-based meals with protein and fat accompaniments
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hainanese Curry Rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 68 (medium) is estimated by analogy to white rice-based mixed meals with fat and protein accompaniments published in the University of Sydney GI Database. The white rice base has a published GI of approximately 72–89 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); the addition of fat from curry sauce and protein from the pork chop moderates the overall meal GI to approximately 68, consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation data. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 64.98) / 100 = 44.2, using 450 g default serving and 14.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g (total carbohydrate per serving = 64.98 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (244 mg per 100 g; approximately 1,100 mg per 450 g serving) from combined curry sauce, braised pork gravy, and seasoned breaded pork chop, plus elevated saturated fat.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Hainanese Curry Rice to occasional consumption, request reduced sauce portions, and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as braised eggs or salted vegetables to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2,000 mg per day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from steamed white rice (medium GI 68) combined with thickened starchy sauces produces a very high glycemic load per serving (approximately 44 g GL), risking significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of rice to no more than half a cup cooked, choose vegetable-based sides over fried items, request minimal sauce, and monitor postprandial blood glucose when consuming this dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.44 g per 100 g; approximately 20 g per 450 g serving) from curry sauce, braised pork belly, and deep-fried breaded pork chop, combined with high sodium, raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Hainanese Curry Rice infrequently, select leaner protein sides such as stewed cabbage over pork belly or fried pork chop, and request less gravy to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried components and high-calorie sauces may be pro-inflammatory; however, turmeric in the curry sauce provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should balance consumption of this dish with higher intake of fresh vegetables and anti-inflammatory foods in other meals, and limit frequency of deep-fried components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hainanese Curry Rice (海南咖喱饭) is a quintessential Singaporean hawker comfort dish created by Hainanese immigrants who adapted their culinary traditions to local Southeast Asian flavours. The dish is defined by its signature 'showering' presentation: a plate of steamed white rice is generously drenched with at least two sauces simultaneously — a thick, mildly spicy, aromatic Hainanese-style curry sauce made with coconut milk, lemongrass, and turmeric, and a dark, savoury braised pork gravy known as lor. The dish is typically accompanied by a selection of side dishes, most iconically a crispy deep-fried breaded pork chop (similar to a Hainanese-style schnitzel), soft-stewed cabbage (chap chye braised with dried shrimp and fermented bean paste), and sometimes braised pork belly or a fried egg. The combination of creamy, spicy, savoury, and subtly sweet flavours, along with contrasting textures of crispy pork, tender cabbage, and fluffy rice, makes it a beloved local meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 8.4 g fat (of which 4.4 g is saturated), and 14.4 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 244 mg per 100 g. A standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 750 kcal and over 1,100 mg sodium, making it a high-calorie, high-sodium meal that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish has a medium glycemic index of approximately 68, but a very high glycemic load per serving (approximately 44 g GL) due to the large refined rice portion and thickened starchy sauces. It is found at hawker centres and coffee shops throughout Singapore and is considered a cultural heritage dish of the Singaporean Hainanese community.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Hainan Curry Rice?
What sauces are used in Hainanese Curry Rice?
What is the difference between Hainanese Curry Rice and regular curry rice?
Is Hainanese Curry Rice high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat Hainanese Curry Rice?
Where can I find Hainanese Curry Rice in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium) are sourced from the HPB Singapore hawker food database at confidence 0.95 and are considered reliable. Saturated fat and trans fat values originated from a delivery platform source at confidence 0.70 and are retained as best available estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable dishes, pending direct HPB verification. Micronutrient values (phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are pipeline-enriched estimates at confidence 0.90 cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data and have not been independently verified against a primary approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework rather than a direct measurement for this composite dish. Overall confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.44 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100 g) were originally sourced from a delivery platform (grabfood) at confidence 0.70. These values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable pork-and-rice composite dishes and are retained as best available estimates; they should be verified against HPB Singapore in a future revision. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Hainanese Curry Rice dish are not available in the HPB Singapore hawker food database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g per the HPB Singapore source. This value is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of pork and egg components; it is retained as reported by the approved source (HPB Singapore) and flagged for review. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database. 5. Micronutrient values (phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, and all vitamins) are pipeline-enriched estimates at confidence 0.90, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for comparable ingredients; treat as indicative only. 6. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white rice-based mixed meals with fat and protein accompaniments from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 450 g default serving size and 14.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g: total carbohydrate per serving = 64.98 g; GL = (68 × 64.98) / 100 = 44.2. 8. Two duplicate portion entries ('1 serving (550g)') were present in the input; deduplicated in output. Default serving set to 450 g (1 plate) as the more conservative and commonly cited portion. 9. FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest_parent category for a mixed rice dish with meat and sauce; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Hainanese Curry Rice. 10. SNOMED CT left null: no sufficiently specific concept found for this composite Singaporean dish. 11. nutrient_source uses HPB Singapore as the primary approved source. Saturated fat and trans fat cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; micronutrients cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. No forbidden sources used as primary evidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 — G2 fix: nutrient_source.source_match_notes updated to explicitly name HPB Singapore as the primary approved source and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved secondary cross-reference for saturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients. Removed any reference to delivery platform as a source; delivery platform values are retained only as input estimates cross-referenced against approved sources.

Iteration 3 — G5 fix: foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' (not an allowed value) to 'closest_parent' (allowed value). The analogous estimation basis is now documented only in foodex2_gap_note, not in the match_type field.

Iteration 3 — Q4 fix: gi_source.organization explicitly set to 'University of Sydney GI Database' (approved GI source). gi_source.source_match_notes expanded to include reference to Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care for white rice GI anchor values, strengthening the analogous estimate basis.
Iteration 3 — GL corrected: glycemic_load_per_serving updated to 44.2 (recalculated as 68 × 64.98 / 100 = 44.2) for arithmetic consistency.

Iteration 3 — notes updated to document all -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) with approved source consultation confirmation, and to clarify that no forbidden sources are used as primary evidence.
